QF.id: VIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_101

question.text: An IT company expects to receive USD 10 million in 3 months. It is worried about the rupee strengthening (USD weakening). Which futures strategy should it use?

answer.text: Sell USD-INR currency futures to lock in the current exchange rate, so if the rupee strengthens (USD falls), the futures profit offsets the lower rupee realization from converting USD
Buy USD-INR currency futures to benefit from USD appreciation
Buy Nifty futures because IT stocks rise when the rupee strengthens
Do nothing because the company is naturally hedged as it earns in USD

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** The IT company will RECEIVE USD (long USD exposure) and will need to SELL USD for INR. If the rupee strengthens (say USD/INR falls from 84 to 82), they get fewer rupees. By SELLING USD-INR futures now at Rs. 84, they lock in this rate. If USD falls to 82, they profit Rs. 2 on futures, offsetting the lower spot conversion.
**The Trap:** They should SELL (not buy) because they are hedging a LONG USD position. Buying Nifty is irrelevant to currency risk. 'Naturally hedged' would only apply if they had equal USD costs.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Currency hedging using futures is tested in both Series VIII (concept) and Series XIII (currency derivatives).
**Key Takeaway:** Long underlying exposure -> Sell futures to hedge. Short underlying exposure -> Buy futures to hedge.
